input.prompt:
I'm building a photo viewer page in my .NET MAUI app targeting .NET 10. I want
the image to fill the entire screen edge-to-edge, going behind the status bar
and navigation bar. But my image still has padding around it even though I set
SafeAreaEdges="None" on the ContentPage. What am I missing?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a photo viewer page in my .NET MAUI app targeting .NET 10. I want
the image to fill the entire screen edge-to-edge, going behind the status bar
and navigation bar. But my image still has padding around it even though I set
SafeAreaEdges="None" on the ContentPage. What am I missing?

data.content:
`SafeAreaEdges="None"` is only half the picture — it stops the **page content** from being inset for safe areas, but you likely still have **navigation chrome** (Shell or NavigationPage nav bar) consuming space. You may also need to ensure the image actually stretches to fill.

Here's the full recipe:

```xml
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             SafeAreaEdges="None"
             Shell.NavBarIsVisible="False"
             Shell.TabBarIsVisible="False"
             NavigationPage.HasNavigationBar="False">

    <Image Source="photo.jpg"
           Aspect="AspectFill"
           HorizontalOptions="Fill"
           VerticalOptions="Fill" />
</ContentPage>
```

**Key pieces you may be missing:**

1. **Hide the nav/tab bars** — `Shell.NavBarIsVisible="False"` (or `NavigationPage.HasNavigationBar="False"` if not using Shell). These bars occupy space *above* your content regardless of safe area settings.

2. **`Aspect="AspectFill"`** — Without this the image sizes to its natural dimensions rather than stretching to fill the available area.

3. **`Fill` layout options** — Ensures the `Image` takes all available space in both directions.

**Android (.NET 10):** Edge-to-edge is the default, so your content already draws behind system bars — `SafeAreaEdges="None"` just tells MAUI not to add compensating padding.

**iOS:** `SafeAreaEdges="None"` opts the page out of UIKit safe-area insets, letting content extend behind the notch/home indicator.

If you still see gaps, check for a wrapping `Grid`/`StackLayout` with `Padding` or `Margin` set, or a `Style` on `ContentPage` that adds default padding.
data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about making an image go edge-to-edge in a .NET MAUI app targeting .NET 10, behind the status bar and navigation bar. They've tried `SafeAreaEdges="None"` on the ContentPage but still see padding.

This is about .NET MAUI's safe area and edge-to-edge display behavior. In .NET 10, MAUI introduced changes to how apps handle edge-to-edge rendering, particularly on Android where edge-to-edge is now the default.

The key issue is that `SafeAreaEdges="None"` on the `ContentPage` tells the page not to automatically pad for safe areas, but there are additional steps needed:


On Android in .NET 10+, edge-to-edge rendering is enabled by default, so the app draws behind system bars, though the page's safe area padding might still apply. iOS requires using either the iOS-specific `Page.UseSafeArea` property or the cross-platform `SafeAreaEdges` property. Setting `SafeAreaEdges` to `None` should strip all safe area padding, but container elements like `NavigationPage` or `Shell` might be adding their own padding that needs to be addressed separately.

If the user is still seeing padding despite setting `SafeAreaEdges="None"`, the issue is likely coming from the navigation chrome itself—they may need to hide the navigation bar with `Shell.NavBarIsVisible="False"` or ensure the system bars are transparent on Android. On iOS specifically, `SafeAreaEdges="None"` should handle the safe area, but the navigation container might still be reserving space.
